topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The blue light of my phone screen felt like a physical weight pressing against my chest. I was scrolling through my feed, mindlessly consuming updates, when a familiar notification popped up. It was from Mr. Harrison, my notoriously strict history teacher. A photo of a slightly blurry campus event. My breath hitched. I quickly minimized the app, my fingers trembling as I checked my notifications again. There. A recent story post from him. A picture taken near the library, and in the background, unmistakably, was me. A small, awkward glimpse of me laughing with a friend. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outwards. I started replaying every interaction we’d ever had in class, scrutinizing every glance, every word. Was he watching? Was he tracking my movements? I felt an intense need to check his profile repeatedly, a compulsion that made my palms sweat. The simple act of opening the app felt like stepping into a trap.

[story 2]

I kept glancing over my shoulder in the hallway, convinced someone was lingering just outside my peripheral vision. It was just the usual afternoon bustle, but my senses were dialed up to an unbearable frequency. Then I saw it: a notification on my private account. Mr. Davies, my English professor, had liked a post I’d made about a challenging assignment. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. I immediately closed the app, shoving the phone deep into my backpack, the material rough against my skin. I walked faster, my steps hurried and jerky, trying to blend into the flow of students. Every face seemed to hold a hidden observation, every casual conversation sounded like coded surveillance. I started avoiding the common areas, preferring the solitude of the empty stairwells, the silence amplifying the frantic buzzing in my ears. I couldn't shake the feeling that I was being cataloged, assessed, like an specimen under a microscope.

[story 3]

The silence of my room was usually a comfort, a sanctuary where my thoughts could settle. Today, it was suffocating. I was reviewing notes, but my eyes kept drifting to my phone, which lay face up on the desk. A message from Professor Evans. It was a direct message, something about a shared interest in 19th-century literature. A small flicker of unease ignited into a full-blown anxiety. I snatched the device up, my fingers fumbling slightly as I navigated to his profile. There, a recent photo of a campus event, and there, undeniably, was me, standing near the refreshment table. My jaw tightened so hard I thought my teeth might crack. I dropped the phone onto the duvet, the sound muffled by the fabric. I curled into a tight ball beneath the covers, pulling my knees to my chest, trying to make myself small, invisible. The air in the room suddenly felt thin, charged with an unseen scrutiny. I stayed frozen, waiting for the next inevitable digital intrusion.
